transcript.text: Hello.
How are you doing?
I'm good.
Hey, guys.
How are you?
Hi, Kirkland.
Oh, you're somewhere nice, huh?
Just at a coffee shop, but it was Dollar General right there, so real I thought you were outside.
was like, oh, look.
Not quite.
Almost.
Clean windows.
Yeah, and, like, the doors open.
Super nice outside today, so.
I have to get out of my dungeon basement sometimes, you know?
Yeah, the heat went on in my house for the first time this fall.
Yeah, got, like, 40-something last night, so it's wonderful.
Potato, potato, there.

Okay, so we have a bunch of questions for you really about setting up PSEO pipelines in general, and this comes from the DeepGram ticket that we talked about on Tuesday, and the questions that you had.
So let me link it to you.
Yeah, this is not the, like, 604, do think it's the one?
Yeah, is 604, yeah.
Yeah.
Okay, perfect.

So we, like, this is kind of, like, the high level that the client was thinking about, but I think Erik and I both, and maybe other people too, so it's good we're recording this, and we can start to, like, put this into documentation, but, like, we're not really sure, kind of, like, what format we need, what we need to ask the client for.
Or of that list of things, I know you said, like, okay, they're publishing, what are they publishing to, and then the languages that they have, and then the code snippets.
But, yeah, like, if you were to say, this ticket is 100% ready, what would it need, what would it look like?
Yeah, I'm going to ramble a little bit, probably, and then we'll get to an answer, but just let me kind of talk out loud, because it's helpful.

So, when I'm thinking about PSEO, programmatic SEO, there are different, I there are different layers of complexity, but let's just assume we're doing, like, the most complex version of this, right, and then work backwards.
Of, like, number one solved is publishing, like, how is that content actually getting published?
Because you're always doing this at scale, you're never doing 20 PSEO pages, so you need to know how you're going to automate publishing.
So, that requires knowing what CMS they're on, and, like, what the data structure of those pages.
So is it, you know, a header with a markdown block of content and that's it?
Or is it something with, you know, code snippets are still marked down, it's not really a big deal.
But like, do we need anything interactive, right?
Is there any, so for Deepgram, for example, do they want some kind of button you can click on the page that's going to translate some text on the page to a different language, right?
Or let you speak into a microphone and display the text on the page?
Are they going to do anything like that?
Yes or no, like that obviously adds a pretty significant layer of complexity in terms of actually delivering it.
So I tend not to recommend doing that at first, because it's like, why put all those engineering resources into a page you don't even know if it's going to work or not?
Right?

So like, in general, to get things started, markdown is easiest, most of the time, if they're on a CMS, like maybe Webflow, or something like that, you may end up not with not just markdown, but you might have a
If of CMS items, so maybe you have a header and then a paragraph, and then you want an image somewhere in the middle of the content, that might also change the data structure.
So, you know, if I'm going build a workflow that's going to do the PSEO, we can kind of, like, make markdown and then translate that into whatever structure it needs to be.
But it's just helpful to understand up front, like, are there any custom elements?
Do we, like, how is it getting uploaded?
All that kind of stuff.
Is that all clear so far?
Does that make sense?
And then the other side of that is thinking about, like, what is the actual content?
Like, how are we going to create it?
So this is, like, what goes into the creation of the PSEO pages.
Do we need any external research?
Are there any proprietary data sources we need to get information from?
So, again, thinking about just some examples, let's say we want to do PSEO for, like,
LLC creation requirements in different states.
That requires a lot of external research.
need to have some research agent go find information and have some way of validating it.
But then like in this case, like Deepgram wants to do a case study or a customer story.
How are we going to integrate that?
Is there just one?
Or, you know, are they going to rotate a random out of 10 different case studies regardless of the page and we just pick one of 10?
Or is there a unique case study for every single page?
And that's going to be related to the content itself.
Like, what does that look like?
Right?
Is it a couple or is it a couple hundred in a big CSV file or something?
And there, that can get like infinitely complex depending on what you're doing, like what the inputs are.
But the biggest delineation, like even if you don't have all the details is being able to say like all the information we need from this can just come from researching the the open web.
Versus, we have some sort of proprietary data from the client that we need to include in each one of these pages, because those are two very different engineering means.
Like, ingesting data is like a separate sort of thing, we also have to get it from the client, and then make sure the right data goes to the right pages, all that kind of stuff.
I've said a bunch of things.
I think that's clear and making sense.
I feel like I'm missing something.
So, the external data, we can, we can like specify which sites it should go to, right?
Correct.
We can now.
Yeah, it's relatively recent, but we can do that for the most part, yeah.
Okay, and we don't need to like create our own database from those sources ahead of time.
We can just say just go to those sites.
Yep.
Yeah, our researcher is pretty, like, really good these days, the agentic one that Daniel put together.
And so, like, you know, if you want to say, let's use, like, the LLC example again, because it's, like, super specific.
Yeah.
You can literally say only use the government, you know, specific resources for these states, or you can even pass only look at these specific URLs or these specific domains.
There might be sometimes it goes a little bit outside of that, but not enough to really matter for the most part.
And that does a really, really good job.
Okay, perfect.
So, and then the other thing would just be like, it's usually helpful, and it'll be necessary at some point, but it's helpful early on to have like an example of what the page is going to look like.
So, like, if you're filing a PSEO ticket, like, just go into Claude and do a mock-up of what the content might look like structure-wise.
Just like one finished page is a really helpful thing to have.
Because then we can say, like, you know, do it.
I said Erik has that.
Yeah, good.
But they were going to send us some wireframes, too, next week.
Okay.
And that's, those are the things where stuff can start to get a little funny, because like, like, a lot of times.
times.
We might have a design wireframe that doesn't think about how the data is going to go into the CMS, and they're a little bit different sometimes.
Again, if the CMS is just H1 markdown block, okay, that's easy enough.
But sometimes maybe they want CTAs embedded throughout the article, each of those need custom text, or they want custom images, or they want, again, some sort of interactive component.
And a lot of the times with SaaS companies in particular, if you want the page to do well, having something interactive is always a really good thing to do.
But it takes more resources and more time to set up in the beginning.
So a lot of what you're doing in that respect is judging, like, you know, how important is it to just get pages live and see how they do, versus, like, you're always weighing those tradeoffs.
And we can always do more complicated things, it's just going to take more time.
And I think one of
The pitfalls you've run into with programmatic is you spend 6, 8, 12 weeks getting the page structure and all the little details perfect.
When really we should have just thrown up some content, put 100 pages up that are kind of close, see what performs, what doesn't, and then, you know, change them later.
Like that's always a thing you can do.
Okay.
So those are a lot of thoughts.
Uh, super helpful question on the language.
So since this is about languages and one of the goals is like global reach.
So if we're publishing in English and talking about translations, could we also publish in another language?
Like, I know we have that in tag for tag, but like, how do we feel about that generally?
Uh, we definitely can.
That does talking about complexity.
That's like an enormous layer of complexity.
I'd say that's even more so.
That just like adding interactive feature, just because we have to do like hreflang tags, and we have to set up either subfolders or subdomains, depending on how they want to do things.
Some folders would probably be preferable.
And then you have to make sure when you start doing other languages, like you kind of want each language subfolder on a website to live within itself.
So you really don't want anything in slash ES linking to anything on the main website or in slash FR or slash DE or whatever, if you can help it.

So like if you're going to localize these pages, you also should have a localized homepage, or at least a localized landing page that they can convert on and the product needs to be localized and the docs need to be localized.
Obviously, you don't have to have all of that, but I'd argue if you just throw a bunch of like, you know, Spanish pages up and
If have no support for Spanish anywhere in your product, that's probably not going to perform super well.
Okay.
Good to know.
Yeah.
But it's totally doable.
Like, I mean, we probably would need it.
If we were going to go that far, we'd probably introduce a new, like, translation workflow or, like, think about that a little bit differently.
But it wouldn't be that hard.
Okay.
I'm just thinking if, like, they wanted to do that as a secondary thing of, like, doing passwords.
I think it's a great idea.
It's just, like, just know that, like, it needs them to do a lot of stuff on their whole website.
That's not just us creating content in those languages.
It's not quite that simple.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Totally.
Okay.
And then we have 11 labs, like, pages as a reference.
So, okay.
So, it sounds like we need to ask them, well, you already told us what we need to ask them about, like, the, where the code snippets are coming from and the case studies and things like that.
Okay.
Yeah, because, like, code snippets.
That's a case where because it's code snippets of their own product, we probably don't want to just like trust the researcher for that.
We'd like to have some like guaranteed good examples, but they probably have that.
I'm sure they have that in their docs, right?
This is how you do it.
So that should be easy.

It's just knowing like, you know, it's helpful if rather than us having to go read through their documentation and try to figure it out, if they can just be like, hey, here's a good example of how we think about this.
Just save us a lot of headache.
Perfect.
Okay.
That's DeepGram.
Erik, we had other questions on...
HyperExponential.
HyperExponential.
That was the MCP versus...
For Framer.
Like they wanted to do a MCP instead of an API.
Framer Publishing.
Yeah, that's it.
Got to look into that.
don't think I can give you a whole lot right now because I looked into Framer stuff a while back.

And really the only way to automate publishing on Framers is to build, I guess an MCP could work, but like the way that it was looked at, but I was looking at it before was you basically have to build like a custom Framer app and put code in your own code base related to how you want that custom app to operate.
So like biggest thing is we build something for Framer.
It only works for that client's Framer.
Like we can't just then go use it everywhere.
We kind of have to like, we could take the learnings and apply it somewhere else, but you would have to kind of rebuild it in their code base from what I can understand.
But Framer, think just launched MCP support like super recently.
So I haven't looked into it a ton.
So that could be interesting.
Like it may make it a lot easier.
I'm not entirely sure yet.
Yeah.
Okay.
That's fine.
Um, and then
Did you set up PSO for Ramp?
I did not do Ramp.
That was Marcus.
Okay, so then that's a question for Marcus.
All right.
Yeah, because there was a ton of stuff with Sanity that he figured out.
That was kind of annoying, but he eventually got there.
Yeah.
And then do you do the ImageGen stuff?
I have done quite a bit of ImageGen stuff, yeah.
Okay, so we had another bug for Understory.
I figured if I have you, I'm just going to throw things at you.
Yeah, hey, that's totally fine.
Knock it all out.
So Understory wants text in their images, and we were having trouble figuring out, like, how to select what that text is.
Like, Katya was playing around with it, and she couldn't, she's going to file a ticket, but, like, she couldn't specify the text that's generated in the images.
Is there a way to do that?
Can you give me, like, is there something in their pipeline?
Perfect.
And I can look at as an example.
Let me see.
I'm looking at their article gen pipeline, and they have like the HTML templates.
I think I understand what you're saying, but I'm going to share my screen.
Yeah.
Just to clarify, we're looking at like these final images controlling the text that's in these graphics.
I mean, you should be able to do that.
The way the workflow typically works, and it takes the content of the article, and then kind of like comes up with some text there.
Are you saying you want to like for every individual row be able to say this is the headline and sub headline I want in my image?
I think so.
I think that would be helpful.
But yeah, like if we could tweak it at this stage, even.
Oh, that's definitely not doable.
Okay.
Just because like it's an image that's generated.

You would basically, I think what you could do is you could set up like a separate input for like hero text and like secondary text or something in the input of the workflow, and then map that in this step as like, you can already put a custom text variable, right?
Um, which I don't know where that's coming from right now.
Maybe it's generated somewhere.
Yeah, okay.
Actually, it's probably taking the title of the row header image.
Yeah, so text is that and then final coverage custom text takes the topic.
Uh, and you could probably rather than sending the content, we could try doing something of like, just put some text to say what you want.
That bottom text to be.
But right now, like, it's going to take that text as whatever the topic is.
Okay.
And then cut it off for length, right?
So, because the AI is making all those decisions behind the scenes.
Right.
So, which step was that input in?
This final cover images.

So, yeah, you're probably going to have a bit of a headache trying to get this to, because there are a couple of places where, like, this custom text, right, that doesn't just automatically get added.
That's dependent on what your HTML template looks like.
And then the subtext is dependent on the article content and a bunch of prompting that happens.
And then there's, in the context artifacts, this HTML template that looks unreadable in dark mode, but I don't
I'm not going to go back and switch it.
This is all of the, like, what that HTML looks like.
And so there's definitely ways to have some influence over that text.
You might have to, like, from time to time, maybe run it twice or, like, make a tweak to the topic and then run it.
You know what I mean?
Like, you could, for example, change this.
I'm just going to do this for a second because it's not going to change anything.
But let's say my title here, save it, and then rerun your final cover images.
And that should, I guess not.
Oh, it's running.
But that should then put, like, my title here as that top text.
And then the subtitle is going to be coming from just whatever the ad thinks it wants it to be from the article content.
But I think you could do some tweaks to the HTML template and the, like, mapping of everything to have more control over that.
very much.
much.
But you can't, like, for example, just type in exactly what you want in both of those places right now.
Yeah.
Okay.
But if that was, like, important enough, I'm sure we could make an adjustment to the workflow to be able to do something like that.
Okay, yeah.
So it sounds like filing a ticket and then you guys coordinating that will be good.
That is a blocker for us publishing for them right now.
So I'll just – we'll put that as high when Katya puts it in.
Yeah.
Okay.
Yeah, is it just – and the main issue is what would they want it to say that it's not saying?
Like, do they have very – I don't actually know.
I think she's just – yeah.
Because that would be my only thought is, like, we can give more control, but, like, what do you need the control for?
Like, is there a way you can solve it in the meantime outside of, like, rewriting the workflow?
Maybe, maybe not.
But I think that's a worthwhile question to ask.
Yeah, she said she spent some time messing.
With it, I don't know what she did, but like, yeah.
I think she did this custom text, and now hopefully, yeah.
So see, like I changed the input topic, and now I can change this top text.
Okay.
And then there was no subtitle.
Oh, no, there is subtitle, and then the subtitle is just going to be randomly determined based on the content of the article.
Yeah.

So, like, actually, I mean, just because I'm here as something to try, you could, we could say, like, for article content, this is, I know it's called article content, but like, we can just name this pipeline.input.subtext.
And then up here, add a new input for subtext, UI text, I bring, Dave, let me just, I'm just going to try.
For subtext, you probably want to use the subtext of this is a very cool slide.
Or my slide is great.
If I say both of those, like really choose one of the two at random, because then you could just like give it some options and like kind of see which one you like or whatever.
I don't know if this is going to work, but we can wait 90 seconds and find out.
Yeah, totally.
Okay, cool.
I feel like I had one more question for you, but now I can't think of it.
Erik, anything else you want to ask an engineer?
I don't know.
Do we have anything on Bubble we wanted to ask?
Oh, I was just looking that up.
No.
No.
Oh, I think ghost does take LLMS TXT.
Okay.
So, Erik, you can file a ticket for the LLMS TXT pipeline for Bubble.
They use ghost as a CMS?
Yeah.
That's crazy.
I haven't heard that name in so long.
We have another client that's on WordPress.
Fairly large.
Dude, ClickUp runs their whole blog on WordPress, though, believe it or not.
I mean, this is their entire website.
4,000 articles.
That's crazy.
Let's see if this did anything or if just broke itself.
It's still, yeah.
Yeah.
It didn't quite like what I did, because there's some sort of prompting that's telling it to write those subtitles.
So even though, like, I gave it specific ones, it still kind of did its own thing.
But, I mean, at least for the time being, maybe you can, like, if you actually wrote a good subtitle and didn't just give this, like, to anyone, like, maybe it would work better.
Okay.
And I'll put the actual write topic back in this now, so you don't see my title here, but.
I guess on the LMS.txt, like, how do we, how do we actually use those, those files?
They just need to be hosted on the client's website somewhere.
So, like, the URL would be, you know, so-to-such, such-and-such, dot, slash, LMS.txt.
Uh, and then.
It's usually just a giant TXT file.
Depending on their CMS and stuff, there are different ways of, like, getting that there.
But that's all.
Like, HockeyStack wanted, they asked, like, do you do it for each blog post?
And I'm like, I do not believe so.
Oh, God.
No, I don't think so.
So there are different people.
I mean, look, there's no right way to do LLMS at TXT yet.
Like, it's not even a real thing.
People have just started doing it.
Everybody's doing it a little bit differently.

Usually, you'll find, for the most part, like, they're used more for, like, So, like, Firecrawl, for example, has, like, this, which is essentially just a markdown file with, like, markdown links to pages with what the topic is for each of those pages.
But this isn't even all of their pages.
It's just, like, some of their blogs and some of the more relevant ones.
And then some of their websites, don't know if Firecrawl has one, they'll have, like, an LLMS-full.

Yeah, where this is like all the content of all of those pages that are linked in the other one, just in their full raw markdown, which sometimes is a little insane depending on how many pages you're working with, right?
Because that might be like, I mean, how many tokens is that?
Yeah, it's 150,000 tokens in that LLM's dashboard.
So if you like, if you're just passing that in an LLM, that's not going to do anything.

The only way that's going to work is if it has some way of ingesting that, and then doing some sort of like retrieval with that giant document, which, if you're using like Claude or ChatGPT in app, sure.
But not if you're just like calling through an API and saying, hey, go, go crawl this URL, have fun with most of your context window eaten up by stuff that's not relevant.
But I would say, like, a good place to start is that first kind of, like, topic URL linked with maybe a little sentence afterwards.
I think we have a workflow that takes a sitemap and roughly generates something like that.
But I forget because I didn't work on it.
Okay.
So, Andy, you said you submitted a ticket for levels to have one of those?
Yeah, on STXD, yeah.
Okay.
Thank you, Kirkland.
Really appreciate your time.
Of course.
Thanks, guys.
Bye.
Bye.
Bye.
Bye.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[Discuss PSEO pipeline setup, image generation, and LLMs.txt implementation for various clients.](https://fathom.video/share/Uh25jHQPDiS6Bg1nCSzwNbi6VRbtPMzG?tab=summary&timestamp=0.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [PSEO complexity varies; publishing automation and content creation are key considerations](https://fathom.video/share/Uh25jHQPDiS6Bg1nCSzwNbi6VRbtPMzG?tab=summary&timestamp=149.0)
  - [Image generation with custom text requires workflow adjustments](https://fathom.video/share/Uh25jHQPDiS6Bg1nCSzwNbi6VRbtPMzG?tab=summary&timestamp=597.0)
  - [LLMs.txt implementation is not standardized; typically involves a markdown file with page links and topics](https://fathom.video/share/Uh25jHQPDiS6Bg1nCSzwNbi6VRbtPMzG?tab=summary&timestamp=667.0)

## Topics

### PSEO Pipeline Setup

  - [Publishing automation is crucial for scale (100+ pages)](https://fathom.video/share/Uh25jHQPDiS6Bg1nCSzwNbi6VRbtPMzG?tab=summary&timestamp=163.0)
  - [Consider CMS type and data structure (e.g., markdown, custom elements)](https://fathom.video/share/Uh25jHQPDiS6Bg1nCSzwNbi6VRbtPMzG?tab=summary&timestamp=173.0)
  - [Content creation may involve external research or proprietary data](https://fathom.video/share/Uh25jHQPDiS6Bg1nCSzwNbi6VRbtPMzG?tab=summary&timestamp=288.0)
  - [Localization adds significant complexity (hreflang tags, subfolders)](https://fathom.video/share/Uh25jHQPDiS6Bg1nCSzwNbi6VRbtPMzG?tab=summary&timestamp=600.0)
  - [Example page mockup is helpful for structure visualization](https://fathom.video/share/Uh25jHQPDiS6Bg1nCSzwNbi6VRbtPMzG?tab=summary&timestamp=440.0)

### Image Generation for Understory

  - [Current workflow uses topic as custom text for images](https://fathom.video/share/Uh25jHQPDiS6Bg1nCSzwNbi6VRbtPMzG?tab=summary&timestamp=1033.0)
  - [Adjusting text requires changes to HTML template and mapping](https://fathom.video/share/Uh25jHQPDiS6Bg1nCSzwNbi6VRbtPMzG?tab=summary&timestamp=1064.0)
  - [Potential solution: Add separate inputs for hero text and secondary text](https://fathom.video/share/Uh25jHQPDiS6Bg1nCSzwNbi6VRbtPMzG?tab=summary&timestamp=1244.0)
  - [Full custom text control would need workflow rewrite](https://fathom.video/share/Uh25jHQPDiS6Bg1nCSzwNbi6VRbtPMzG?tab=summary&timestamp=1540.0)

### LLMs.txt Implementation

  - [File should be hosted on client's website (e.g., domain.com/llms.txt)](https://fathom.video/share/Uh25jHQPDiS6Bg1nCSzwNbi6VRbtPMzG?tab=summary&timestamp=1431.0)
  - [Two common formats:](https://fathom.video/share/Uh25jHQPDiS6Bg1nCSzwNbi6VRbtPMzG?tab=summary&timestamp=1461.0)
    1.  Markdown file with links to pages and topics
    2.  Full content of pages in raw markdown (can be token-heavy)
  - [Starting point: topic-URL links with brief descriptions](https://fathom.video/share/Uh25jHQPDiS6Bg1nCSzwNbi6VRbtPMzG?tab=summary&timestamp=1561.0)
  - [Existing workflow can generate from sitemap](https://fathom.video/share/Uh25jHQPDiS6Bg1nCSzwNbi6VRbtPMzG?tab=summary&timestamp=1569.0)

### Framer Publishing

  - [Previous method required custom Framer app in client's codebase](https://fathom.video/share/Uh25jHQPDiS6Bg1nCSzwNbi6VRbtPMzG?tab=summary&timestamp=786.0)
  - [Recent MCP support may simplify the process (needs investigation)](https://fathom.video/share/Uh25jHQPDiS6Bg1nCSzwNbi6VRbtPMzG?tab=summary&timestamp=829.0)

## Next Steps

  - File high-priority ticket for Understory image text control
  - Erik to submit ticket for LLMs.txt pipeline for Bubble
  - Investigate Framer's new MCP support for potential simplification
  - Consider creating documentation on PSEO setup based on this discussion

action_items.description: File high-priority ticket for Understory image text customization issue
File ticket for LLMS.txt pipeline for Bubble (Ghost CMS)
